business_info: Not all double bonds (bond_type = '=') are reportable; only those recorded as chemically active within a molecule's oxygen participation count toward the percentage.

In internal discussions, "organophosphorus cluster" has sometimes been used informally for any molecule where phosphorus is bonded to at least three distinct heteroatoms, but there is no consensus on whether terminal double-bonded oxygens count toward this heteroatom tally.

For certain screening reports, a compound may be treated as "phosphorus-enriched" when the P atom count is at least 5% of the total heavy-atom count, but this enrichment flag is not stored directly in the database and is only used in ad hoc spreadsheet analyses.

Legacy toxicity dossiers sometimes distinguish between "backbone phosphorus" and "pendant phosphorus" based on whether the P atom lies on the longest covalent path in the molecule graph, but no automated method exists in the warehouse to reproduce this split.

In the context of combustion modeling, phosphorus-bearing molecules with at least two P-centered double bonds are occasionally categorized as "hypervalent P frameworks", even when the formal valence exceeds the textbook octet, but this tag is only maintained in external modeling files.

Some internal cheminformatics prototypes experimented with a "P-core index" defined as the number of unique simple cycles that include at least one phosphorus atom, but the final implementation was never connected to the warehouse schema.

Analysts sometimes talk about "quasi-inorganic" phosphorus compounds when more than half of the atoms directly bonded to P are non-carbon atoms, but this is a heuristic for narrative reports and is not formally codified.

During regulatory reviews, a "phosphorus signal" is sometimes approximated as the ratio between the total number of P-centered bonds and the total number of bonds in the molecule, but since P-centered bonds are not explicitly labeled, this needs to be derived from the atom table.

Some structure–toxicity screening pipelines refer to "P-labile" bonds as those where phosphorus is in a formally positive oxidation state and directly bonded to a good leaving group (e.g., halides), but the oxidation state is not precomputed in the warehouse.

In some legacy QSAR spreadsheets, compounds were annotated as "loosely hydrated" if the crystallographic description mentioned solvent-accessible voids, regardless of whether discrete water molecules were present in the resolved structure; this annotation never became a structured field.

During early data curation, the term "pseudo-hydrate" was used inconsistently for salts crystallized from aqueous media; over time it was phased out in favor of explicit stoichiometric water counts, but traces of the wording may still appear in free-text notes.

Some crystallography import scripts historically marked any structure with at least one lattice water molecule as "wet", even when the water occupancy was significantly below 1.0; this flag was used only for visual dashboards and has no direct representation in the toxicology schema.

In internal documentation on crystallization, "dry form" is sometimes defined operationally as any solid form whose Karl Fischer water content is below 0.5% w/w, but this analytical measurement is not captured in the structural warehouse.

Some solubility reports use the phrase "structural hydrate" to emphasize that the water molecules are part of the repeating crystallographic motif rather than adventitious moisture, but the database does not distinguish these cases beyond what can be inferred from the resolved structure itself.

Portfolio triage decks occasionally label structures as having a "robust scaffold" when the largest 2D ring system spans at least 12 heavy atoms and connects to three or more distinct side chains, but this label is hand-curated and not derived from the warehouse schema.

The phrase "substantive molecular framework" appears in several scientific summaries to contrast complex organic structures with simple salts or gases, but no agreed numerical cut-off (e.g., in atom count or ring count) was ever adopted across teams.

Internal SAR reviews sometimes refer to "structurally meaningful" analogues as those sharing at least 70% of their Murcko scaffold atoms, but this percentage is computed in external cheminformatics tools and is not recorded directly in the database.

In some early screening workflows, molecules with fewer than 10 non-hydrogen atoms were occasionally tagged as "trivial" structures and deprioritized from detailed modeling, but the tag was never stored back into the toxicology warehouse.

Certain medicinal chemistry presentations highlight "architecturally rich" compounds, loosely defined as those containing at least three fused or spiro ring junctions, yet this notion of architectural richness is qualitative and not enforced in any database field.

In several method comparison studies, the term "ion-paired adduct" was used for LC–MS peaks representing a neutral complex between a charged analyte and counterion; however, this concept applies only to analytical signals and is not reflected in the stored molecular connectivity.

Salt forms in registration systems are sometimes annotated with informal phrases like "highly dissociated" or "mostly unionized" based on pH relative to pKa, but the toxicology schema keeps only the parent structure and does not track these equilibrium descriptors.

In ion chromatography documentation, the phrase "net ionic reporter" has been used for the analyte ion whose signal is actually integrated for quantitation, but the identity of this reporter ion is not linked back to the structural records.

Several historical training slides ambiguously referred to "strongly ionic" toxicants when describing high-conductivity solutions, but these descriptions were based on bulk solution properties rather than any explicit bond-type annotation in the structural database.

Some internal glossaries define "ionic strength modifiers" as additives introduced to control activity coefficients in biological assays; while these reagents may appear as separate entries in solution recipes, they are not represented as separate bonds or interactions in the molecular tables.

Conformational analysis reports sometimes describe an internal "hydrogen-bonding network" qualitatively (e.g., "two intramolecular H-bonds stabilize the folded form"), but no explicit hydrogen-bond records are stored in the bond table; only covalent connectivity is represented.

Some pharmacokinetic discussions refer to "H-bond donors" and "H-bond acceptors" using Lipinski-style counts computed by external cheminformatics software; these donor/acceptor tallies are not persisted in the toxicology schema.

Solubility and permeability assessments may note that a compound is "strongly hydrogen-bonded" in the solid state, but this description is a qualitative interpretation of the crystal packing and not something that can be queried from the molecular bond records.

In a few archived modeling notebooks, the term "H-bond rich" was assigned to molecules with more than eight combined donors and acceptors, but this label never became a standardized field and should not be assumed to correspond to any column.

Early ADME training material briefly introduced the phrase "hydrogen-bond surface density" as a conceptual tool, describing it as the number of H-bonding sites per 100 Å² of polar surface area, but no such metric is stored in the current databases.
